title: Livestock medication storage and disposal
subtitle: Keeping the medicines cabinet legal, safe, and effective.
excerpt: A livestock medicines cabinet must meet a specific set of legal and practical requirements: medicines stored correctly stay effective; medicines stored incorrectly are useless or dangerous; and medicines disposed of incorrectly damage the environment and can trigger legal action. The rules are straightforward once you know them.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Medicines for Veterinary Use guidance, gov.uk. Veterinary Medicines Directorate (VMD), guidance for farmers and keepers, gov.uk/vmd. Environment Agency, controlled drugs and waste disposal guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A veterinary medicine that requires a veterinary prescription before it can be obtained or used. Antibiotics, most anti-parasitics, and some vaccines are POM-V in the UK. They cannot be purchased over the counter.

content.text: Any keeper of sheep, goats, pigs, or cattle must keep a medicine record and store medicines correctly. APHA or Trading Standards may check the medicines cabinet at any inspection.
Keep all animal medicines in a locked cabinet or box. Children and animals must not be able to reach them.
Products that need refrigerating (some vaccines) must be kept at the temperature on the label, typically 2 to 8°C. Keep a dedicated medicines fridge separate from food.
Check expiry dates at the start of each season and before each use. Expired medicines must not be used on food-producing animals.

For food-producing animals (sheep, pigs, goats, cattle, poultry), you must record every medicine treatment: product name, batch number, date of treatment, animal(s) treated, dose given, and withdrawal period.
must include the prescribing vet's details. Records must be kept for a minimum of 5 years and produced on request to APHA or Trading Standards.

Some medicines are non-prescription and available from an agricultural merchant or online supplier. Many wormers, some vaccines, and ectoparasite treatments fall into this group. POM-V medicines need a vet prescription. The vet must have seen the animals or hold the herd under a veterinary health plan.

Return expired or unused medicines to a registered vet practice or a licensed waste contractor. Do not pour liquid medicines down the drain, many antibiotics and anthelmintics persist in waterways and harm aquatic invertebrates. Sharps (used needles and syringes) go into a yellow sharps bin, available free or cheaply from your vet. When full, the sharps bin can be returned to the vet or picked up by a licensed medical waste contractor.
